title: ASML Denies EUV in China, White House Writes AI Security Rules with Anthropic
subhead: ASML publicly denied shipping an EUV lithography tool to China after US Commerce Secretary Lutnick raised concerns, the White House and Anthropic are collaborating on AI model security standards after export controls forced its top model offline, and the G7 launched a critical minerals alliance to counter China's 70% refining dominance.

sections.heading: ASML Denies EUV Tool Reached China After US Raises Alarm
sections.body:
Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick told ASML executives in a series of recent meetings that the US is concerned one of the company's most advanced EUV lithography machines may have reached China, Bloomberg first reported. ASML pushed back firmly on Friday, stating it had never shipped an EUV tool to China and calling the concerns based on 'rumors' that are 'inaccurate,' according to Reuters.

The episode underscores a persistent anxiety in Washington: that export controls on semiconductor manufacturing equipment, however tight on paper, may not be airtight in practice. EUV machines — each costing over $200 million — are the single most critical bottleneck in advanced chipmaking. ASML is the only company in the world that builds them, and the Netherlands has barred their sale to China since 2019. Whether or not a machine actually slipped through, the fact that the US Commerce Secretary is raising it directly with ASML leadership signals that the enforcement architecture around chip equipment is under active stress-testing.
sections.whyItMatters: If EUV technology has found a back door into China, the entire Western semiconductor sanctions regime has a major hole. If it hasn't, Lutnick's pressure on ASML shows Washington is moving from passive export control lists to active verification — and that the Dutch company is now squarely in the enforcement crosshairs.

sections.heading: White House and Anthropic Shift from Export Controls to AI Security Standards
sections.body:
Weeks after the Trump administration forced Anthropic to pull its Mythos and Fable 5 models offline over security concerns, the White House has shifted to collaborating with Anthropic on AI security rules, according to POLITICO and Wired. Rather than continuing a purely punitive approach, the two sides are now working on a framework to evaluate security vulnerabilities in frontier AI models.

A June 2 executive order established a voluntary pre-release review process for the most powerful AI models, CryptoBriefing reported. The shift from forced model takedowns to technical standards-setting represents a pragmatic recognition: export controls alone can't govern software models the way they govern physical chip equipment. The question is whether a voluntary framework with one company creates a real standard — or just a precedent that other labs can ignore.
sections.whyItMatters: The US is groping toward a regulatory model for frontier AI that doesn't yet exist. Anthropic's cooperation gives the White House technical credibility, but also creates an awkward dynamic: the company the government just punished is now helping write the rules. Whether this becomes a real governance framework or a one-off arrangement depends on whether other labs sign on.

sections.heading: G7 Launches Critical Minerals Alliance, Trump Pricing Plan Faces Skepticism
sections.body:
G7 leaders meeting in Evian, France issued a declaration creating a strategic alliance on critical minerals, committing to 'coordinating efforts within the G7 and with partner countries to establish and develop the necessary processing and industrial capacities for diversification,' as reported by OilPrice.com. The declaration expressed concern about 'the use of non-market policies and practices and economic coercion, including arbitrary export restrictions' — language clearly aimed at Beijing.

Separately, Trump's critical minerals pricing plan — which would establish a Western trading bloc with price floors for rare earths — is meeting resistance from skeptical G7 partners and a divided industry, Reuters reported. Meanwhile, China controls roughly 70% of critical mineral refining capacity, according to IEA data cited in the G7 discussions, and has increased its market share in recent years despite Western diversification efforts.

A new study published in Environmental Science and Ecotechnology by researchers from the Chinese Academy of Sciences, Peking University, and the University of Southern Denmark found that the global cobalt supply chain's 'avalanche network' of potential failures is approximately four times denser than the underlying physical trade network, meaning traditional country-by-country risk assessments dramatically underestimate vulnerability.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 is moving from statements to institutional architecture on critical minerals, but Trump's price floor proposal is struggling for support. The gap between the alliance's ambition and China's actual 70% refining dominance remains enormous — and new research suggests the supply chain is even more fragile than policymakers assume.

sections.heading: VivaTech 2026: Europe's AI Infrastructure Moves from Pledges to Steel
sections.body:
VivaTech's 10th anniversary edition in Paris closed with something previous editions lacked: concrete infrastructure commitments, not just political aspirations. Three announcements stand out:

Mistral AI and Nvidia confirmed the launch of Mistral Compute, a sovereign GPU cloud platform built on 18,000 Grace Blackwell Superchips, already partially operational. Mistral CEO Arthur Mensch said the company is expanding 'from an AI company doing software to a cloud company.' Launch partners include Black Forest Labs, BNP Paribas, and Thales.

Foxconn and Bull announced a partnership to manufacture AI servers at Bull's factory in Angers, France, backed by an initial investment exceeding €120 million. Mistral's 44-megawatt data center in Bruyeres-le-Chatel, financed with $830 million in debt, is actively deploying its first systems.

Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang said the company will increase European AI computing capacity 'by a factor of ten' over the next two years. Europe currently holds less than 5% of global AI infrastructure capacity in key segments, according to McKinsey data cited in the Bull-Foxconn press release.

In the robotics track, European firms like Enchanted Tools (Paris) and Neura Robotics (Germany) emphasized domestic manufacturing — Enchanted Tools' Mirokai robot is at least 60% made in Europe — even as Chinese firms like Unitree and Agibot dominated production volume. About 87% of the 13,000 humanoid robots deployed worldwide last year came from Chinese production lines, per Omdia. Neura Robotics raised $1.4 billion and CEO David Reger called robots 'Europe's last chance' to sustain its economy.
sections.whyItMatters: After years of sovereign AI talk in Europe, 2026 is the year concrete infrastructure starts getting built. But the numbers are stark: less than 5% of global AI compute capacity vs. a tenfold expansion promise. The gap between commitment and capacity remains enormous.

sections.heading: China's AI Price War Deepens as Model Capabilities Converge
sections.body:
A full-scale price war is underway in China's AI sector, SCMP reports. ByteDance launched Seedance 2.0 Mini at 23 yuan ($3.40) per million tokens — half the price of its standard version. Xiaomi cut its MiMo V2.5 model price by 99%. Tencent slashed its Hy-MT2-Pro by nearly 70% on the TokenHub platform. MiniMax AI halved the price of its new M3 model. Alibaba offered 50% off Qwen3.7-Max as part of the 618 midyear sales.

Bank of America Securities noted that 'China's AI model landscape is vibrant and intensely competitive, with limited capability gaps across incumbents.' The convergence of model quality across major Chinese labs means price is becoming the primary battleground — a dynamic that pressures smaller players who lack the capital to sustain losses.
sections.whyItMatters: When frontier model capabilities converge, the fight shifts to pricing — and Chinese labs are racing to the bottom fast enough to kill margins for all but the best-capitalized players. This has implications for global AI competition: Chinese models are becoming absurdly cheap at a time when US export controls are trying to restrict their reach.

sections.heading: Nobel Laureate Jumper Leaves DeepMind for Anthropic; Lula's Adviser Warns of Bipolar AI Race
sections.body:
John Jumper, the Nobel Prize-winning scientist who led the development of AlphaFold at Google DeepMind, announced on Friday he is leaving to join Anthropic — the third senior researcher to depart DeepMind in as many months, according to LiveMint. Jumper's move signals that the AI talent war between big tech and well-funded startups continues to intensify, with frontier labs competing for researchers who can claim landmark breakthroughs.

Meanwhile, at the Forte de Copacabana International Security Conference in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil's top foreign policy adviser Celso Amorim said that 'neither Latin America nor Europe is leading the artificial intelligence race. What we see today are two poles, the United States and China,' SCMP reported. Amorim framed BRICS and the Global South as the alternative path for countries that want to draw capital and technology from both sides without choosing one — but acknowledged the balance is 'harder to keep than it sounds,' particularly after Trump's May state visit to Beijing and his June 2 executive order tightening AI national security rules.
sections.whyItMatters: Jumper's departure shows that even Nobel-caliber talent is increasingly mobile — and that startups like Anthropic can outbid Google for the researchers who define the field. Amorim's framing of a bipolar AI race with most of the world as bystanders is a reminder that the governance gap between the two poles and everyone else keeps widening.

sections.heading: US Marines Plan Permanent Weapons Stockpile in Australia; Singapore Exports Surge on AI Demand
sections.body:
The US Marine Corps is planning its first permanent war-ready weapons stockpile in Australia, with $30 million allocated for warehouses and offices in Victoria state, according to US Navy tender documents reported by SCMP/AFP. The stockpile, expected to reach full capacity by 2028, will be initially housed in Melbourne before moving to the Australian military base at Bandiana. It follows a similar US effort in the Philippines — the first land stockpile in the Asia-Pacific, expected to open this year. Analysts said the US is leveraging Australia's strategic position in the South Pacific, beyond the range of most Chinese missiles, to counter Beijing's military build-up.

In trade data, Singapore's non-oil domestic exports rose 38.4% year-on-year in May — the largest increase in 20 years, per LSEG data — driven by AI-related demand for integrated circuits, disk media products, and PCs. Electronics exports to the US grew 303% and to Taiwan 218.6%. The surge underscores how AI infrastructure demand is reshaping global trade flows, even as the US Trade Representative proposed additional 12.5% tariffs on Singapore's exports over forced-labor concerns.
sections.whyItMatters: The weapons stockpile and the export surge tell the same story from opposite angles: the Indo-Pacific is where military posture and tech supply chains converge. Singapore's 303% electronics export growth to the US shows AI demand is creating new trade dependencies even as strategic stockpiling accelerates.

bottomLine: A weekend where the contradictions of tech geopolitics sharpened: ASML denied an EUV breach but the US clearly isn't taking chances; the White House pivoted from punishing Anthropic to co-writing AI security rules with it; Europe started pouring concrete for sovereign AI infrastructure while still holding less than 5% of global capacity; China's model price war hit 99% discounts that will squeeze smaller labs out of existence; and the G7 talked critical minerals alliances while China still refines 70% of what the world needs. The underlying pattern: every major actor is simultaneously building walls and writing rules, but none of the walls are airtight and none of the rules are settled yet.
sources: Reuters, Bloomberg (via MSN/Yahoo), SCMP, Nikkei Asia, TechTimes, Taipei Times, ScienceDaily, Environmental Science and Ecotechnology, OilPrice.com, POLITICO, Wired, CryptoBriefing, Business Insider, LiveMint, Financial Express, Bank of America Securities, IEA, McKinsey (via Bull-Foxconn), Omdia, Enterprise Singapore, AFP
